title: Reduce the committeeMinSize parameter from 7 to 5

rationale.summary: Vote: YES. For this ParameterChange (active), available evidence supports proceeding, with risk judged manageable under current context. Decisive reason: the proposal substantiates "If Constitutional Committee membership drops below committeeMinSize, governance actions requiring Constitutional Committee approval become ineligible for ratification, including Parameter Changes, Constitution Updates, Treasury Withdrawals, and Hard Forks." and clears the evidence gates. Weighed against it: a material claim is unsupported — "The current Constitutional Committee has 7 members, equal to the current committeeMinSize setting of 7." — so cost or precedent may outweigh the benefit. What could still change this vote to NO: Material counter-evidence on the decisive points above, or failure of the stated safeguards, would trigger a revision. Residual uncertainty: Rule-based system; does not infer unstated intent. Confidence context: score 0.03; confidence 58.0%.
rationale.summary_raw: DRep ratification support is material but below threshold; treated as a modest context signal.
top_fixes: Provide independent evidence for: The current Constitutional Committee has 7 members, equal to the current committeeMinSize setting of 7.

sections.title: Intake
sections.conclusion: Baseline fields and source anchors establish whether the proposal can be reviewed at all.

sections.title: Claims and evidence
findings: Proposal document read by reasoning layer: yes
Requested: Reduce the committeeMinSize protocol parameter from 7 to 5.
Stated amount: not stated in document
Claim (governance, proposer_asserted, high materiality): The current Constitutional Committee has 7 members, equal to the current committeeMinSize setting of 7.

Claim (governance, supported_in_proposal, high materiality): If Constitutional Committee membership drops below committeeMinSize, governance actions requiring Constitutional Committee approval become ineligible for ratification, including Parameter Changes, Constitution Updates, Treasury Withdrawals, and Hard Forks.

Claim (governance, supported_in_proposal, high materiality): Reducing committeeMinSize to 5 would create an operational buffer so governance can continue if one or two members resign or their terms expire, assuming a current 7-member body.

Claim (technical, independently_verifiable, medium materiality): The proposal depends temporally on enactment of another live Parameter Change action for Plutus cost model updates, but states there is no technical interaction between the two parameter changes.

Claim (governance, supported_in_proposal, high materiality): The proposed change does not directly affect the current number of Constitutional Committee members and does not affect the maximum number of members.

Claim (governance, supported_in_proposal, high materiality): With a Constitutional Committee size of 5 and a 2/3 threshold, 4 constitutional votes are needed to meet the threshold and 2 unconstitutional votes can prevent the threshold.
missing: Independent evidence for: The current Constitutional Committee has 7 members, equal to the current committeeMinSize setting of 7.
sections.conclusion: Each claim must map to replayable public evidence; proposer assertions alone are not enough for confidence.

sections.title: Risk review
sections.conclusion: Risk is not a side note; unmitigated execution or governance risk can dominate an otherwise attractive proposal.

sections.title: Counterargument pass
findings: Strongest YES: the proposal substantiates "If Constitutional Committee membership drops below committeeMinSize, governance actions requiring Constitutional Committee approval become ineligible for ratification, including Parameter Changes, Constitution Updates, Treasury Withdrawals, and Hard Forks." and clears the evidence gates.

Strongest NO: a material claim is unsupported — "The current Constitutional Committee has 7 members, equal to the current committeeMinSize setting of 7." — so cost or precedent may outweigh the benefit.
Strongest hold: if claims cannot be tied to replayable evidence, abstaining avoids overclaiming certainty.
sections.conclusion: A defensible rationale must show the best opposing case before it reaches a vote.

sections.title: Synthesis
findings: Final vote must be derived from completed sections above, not from a prose summary.
The public rationale should name the decisive section and the strongest counterargument.
sections.conclusion: The vote is only credible if the assessment tree shows enough work for a skeptical delegator to audit.
